In the dawn-time, spirits were mere echoes of the wind, by-products of human minds seeking patterns in the shadows. But as the ice retreated twelve millennia ago, these scattered whispers evolved. They became the Great Watchers—increasingly potent moralizing agents who peered into the hearts of men, demanding virtue and punishing deceit.

To bind growing tribes, the Watchers required psychologically active rituals—rhythms of fire and ordeal. These were credible displays of faith; no soul would endure such costs for a lie. This forged an unbreakable trust among strangers. While fragmented groups faltered, the faithful flourished with high fertility, their settlements teeming with life.

When tribes met in the heat of intergroup competition, the worshippers of the Watchers moved as one body. Through conflict and expansion, they eclipsed those who stood alone. Neighbors, witnessing their prosperity, began group copying, adopting the potent gods and rituals of the victors.

Over long cultural time, these beliefs—initially accidental mental sparks—were shaped into the pillars of civilization. The Watchers grew into cosmic judges, ensuring that those who feared the heavens would inherit the earth. Thus, the myth of the moral god spread, turning small bands into vast empires through the iron bond of shared sacred law.

The gods did not just watch; they commanded the spread of their people, ensuring that the followers of the Moralizing One inherited the earth.
